--- 
title: "Statistics For Science Extension"
author: "Michael Dear"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
site: bookdown::bookdown_site
documentclass: book
bibliography: [book.bib, packages.bib]
# url: your book url like https://bookdown.org/yihui/bookdown
# cover-image: path to the social sharing image like images/cover.jpg
description: |
  These are the notes for the statistics section of the NSW Extension Science (SCIX) course.
link-citations: yes
# github-repo: rstudio/bookdown-demo
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message=FALSE, comment="") # remove code and ## from output
```

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(GGally)
```

# Introduction

These notes are a practical introduction to statistics for students of the NSW Science Extension course. The emphasis is on learning through examples and problem-solving.

## What Is Statistics and What Does It Do?

There is no one simple definition for what statistics is and what it does.

"Statistics uses samples to explain and predict"

"Statistics is a set of techniques for collecting and analysing data"

"Statistics tells the story of the population being studied"

However, it can be said that statistics is fundamental to all stages of scientific research.


## Examples of Statistical Problems

Most scientific research questions will rely heavily on statistical techniques:

* What is the average girth (circumference) of trees in  a forest?
* What is the rate of decline in seabird populations in Australian coastal waters? 
* Does a new vaccine reduce the number of deaths from influenza? 
* Predict the rise in temperature by 2050 in different regions of Australia.

## A More Detailed Example

Forestry scientists would like to know the average girth (circumference) of trees in a forest. There are too many trees to measure them all, so they plan to take a random sample of 200 trees. They need to use statistics to determine the size and structure of the sample. After collecting the data, they complete a statistical analysis to estimate that, to a high level of confidence, the average tree girth is between 175cm and 225cm. They then write a report using statistical tables and plots to communicate their results.

## Scientific Studies

A scientific study has the following steps:

* Research question(s)
* Study design - experimental or observational?
* Data collection
* Data analysis
* Report

## Study Design

There are two basic types of scientific study:

>**Observational study** - variables are not controlled e.g. the health records of 1000 people who had taken Drug X were reviewed to determine the side effects.

>**Experimental study** - variables are controlled e.g. Plots A and B are next to each other. Plot A receives fertiliser and Plot B receives none.

Observational studies can show *associations* between variables, but experimental studies can show *causal* relationships.

>Observational study $\rightarrow$ association -  “A varies as B varies”  
>Experimental study $\rightarrow$ causation - “A causes B”


## Population and Sample

>**Population** - all members of the group being studied e.g. all the Magpies in NSW. Population values are called **parameters**.

>**Sample** - a subset of the population being studied e.g. 1000 magpies chosen at random locations across NSW. Sample values are called **statistics**.

## Symbols For Common Values

Value|Population Parameter|Sample Statistic
-----|--------------------|---------------- 
|Mean|$\mu$|$\bar{x}$
|Standard Deviation|$\sigma$|$s$
|Proportion|$p$|$\hat{p}$|


## Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

>**Descriptive statistics** describe data e.g. mean, median, standard deviation of a data set.

>**Inferential statistics** estimate population parameters from a sample.

Statistical analysis usually begins with descriptive statistics, then moves on to inferential statistics.

------

## Extension - Spreadsheets and Statistical Programming Languages

Spreadsheets are handy for recording and exploring small data sets. However, the capabilities of spreadsheets for statistical analysis are limited. Larger projects are best completed using a statistical programming language such as R. Analyses completed using computer coding have many advantages, such as the ability to handle very large data sets, a greater variety of plots, greater control over plot output, and the availability of sophisticated modelling techniques. Also, a coding script becomes a reproducible record of the steps completed during the project. The disadvantage of coding is the time taken to learn a language, although there are numerous training resources and forums available for free on the internet.

These notes, including plots, tables, and most diagrams, were created using the R programming language.

R Core Team (2025). _R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing_. R Foundation for
  Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria. [https://www.R-project.org/](https://www.R-project.org/).

------

## Activity

Write definitions for each term:

* Population
* Parameter 
* Sample
* Statistic 
* Experimental study
* Causation
* Observational study
* Association
* Descriptive statistics
* Inferential statistics
